control.answer:
This is Matt Wolfe's weekly AI news roundup (38:40, 'AI News: GPT-5.6 and the new Super App are a Massive Leap!'). Main stories, in order:

1) GPT-5.6 launch (the week's biggest story, 00:13-03:21): OpenAI's new flagship family (Sol / Terra / Luna tiers, per the blog page at 01:28), which he says feels like it 'should have been GPT-6' (00:57). Demo showcase (Saltwind regatta game at 01:28-01:35, Clockwork Village, slide decks at 01:56-02:02), benchmarks (GDPval, browser/computer use, GPQA at 02:10-02:40), and it takes the top 4 spots on 'BuseyBench' (the Gary Busey SVG portrait benchmark shown in frames at 02:46-03:04), with cost/speed numbers at 03:09-03:21.

2) The new 'super app' — ChatGPT Work / unified ChatGPT app (03:35-12:53): Codex app + ChatGPT app + Atlas browser merged into one app, with Work vs Codex modes, a Sites feature (hosted websites on chatgpt.site), a side-chat panel, and a personal-assistant demo (control tower, second-brain visualizer).

3) Sponsored segment: Hyper Agent from the Airtable company (12:54-16:11) — a team-of-agents product with a new community skills marketplace (frames at 13:23, 14:00); he demos his own B-roll generator skill that routes prompts to VO or hyperframes (14:07-14:12) and produced a 'Codex Micro / OpenAI's first hardware' B-roll reel (frames at 14:56-15:24).

4) GPT-Live voice mode (16:11-18:38): new ChatGPT voice with natural interruption both ways and real-time in-room translation (English-Hindi example at 16:35-16:56); live on-mic demo where it holds back until told to respond (17:26-18:38).

5) xAI's Grok 4.5 (18:45-23:23): surprisingly competitive on Deep SWE and Terminal Bench (19:57-20:19 compares it to Fable and GPT-5.6), a solar-system animation demo (20:31), a free CLI you can install locally (20:45-21:05), BuseyBench rank ~#21 (21:36, frame at 25:20), an 'impress me' star-map website (21:41-22:58), and API pricing of $2/$6 per million tokens (23:05-23:11).

6) Meta's Muse Spark 1.1 (23:26-26:14): a multimodal reasoning model from Meta Superintelligence Labs (frame at 24:10-24:11) roughly on par with the previous generation (Terminal Bench 80 vs Opus 4.8's 82.7 and GPT-5.5's 83.4; Deep SWE 53.3, at 23:41-23:59), $20 free credits (24:46), huge visual leap from Llama Scout on the BuseyBench timeline (25:01-25:18), and an 'impress me' website demo (25:50-26:14).

7) Meta's Muse Image (27:40-29:04): new image-generation model, free in Meta AI, controversially able to generate images of anyone you @mention via Instagram (27:52-27:59; Mr. Beast on a pile of pickles at 28:44's frame); an opt-out setting is still rolling out (28:49-29:02).

8) Quick Fable comparison (26:16-27:38): Claude Fable didn't launch this week, but he shows its 'impress me' site 'Lumen', a small museum of computational art, which he says still won that test (26:27).

9) Anthropic news (29:28-32:44): Claude Fable 5 access extended from July 7 to July 12 (29:31-29:36) and weekly limits reset on July 9, the day GPT-5.6 launched (30:06-30:16); Claude Co-work coming to mobile and web with cloud execution (30:26-30:57); a new 'Reflect' usage-recap feature likened to Spotify Wrapped (31:13-31:37); and 'J-Space' interpretability research on the model's 'subconscious' (31:40-32:44; the 'A global workspace in language models' page in the frame at 32:24).

10) Google Photos video remix (32:44-33:01): sharable AI-stylized video clips using Gemini Omni, on Google AI Plus/Pro/Ultra plans.

11) ByteDance's Seedream 5.0 Pro image model (33:01-34:00): strong at dense infographics, interactive circle-and-draw editing, Photoshop-like layers, and photorealism (frames at 33:08-33:41); playable in Leonardo, where he made a J-Space infographic and a Gary Busey portrait (33:43-34:00).

12) A joke plug for the relaunched Future Tools website as 'the biggest news of the week' (29:04-29:10), plus closing reflections comparing Fable vs GPT-5.6 as daily drivers (35:10-36:42), including that GPT-5.6 found security vulnerabilities Fable missed on his new site (36:18-36:38).
control.evidence_used: Full caption transcript plus all 100 frames (BuseyBench leaderboard pages, OpenAI/Meta/Anthropic/ByteDance blog pages, ChatGPT app UI, Hyper Agent UI)

control.answer:
Concrete, non-hype facts about GPT-5.6 from this video's evidence:

What it is: OpenAI's new flagship model family, released out of preview on Thursday July 9 (18:36-18:38, 30:06-30:12). It ships in three sizes — Sol (flagship/most powerful), Terra (balanced), Luna (most cost-efficient) — per the OpenAI blog page on screen at 01:28, plus Pro variants (Sol Pro, Terra Pro, Luna Pro appear on the BuseyBench leaderboard frames at 02:46-03:04 and 21:14) and effort settings; in the app a simple slider runs from '5.6 Terra Light' at the bottom to '5.6 Soul Ultra' at the top (05:47-05:58).

Cost (API, per 1M tokens): Sol $5 input / $30 output; Terra $2.50 / $15; Luna $1 / $6 (23:11-23:23, matching the OpenAI pricing page in the frame at 23:12). That same page states prompt-cache support: cache writes billed at 1.25x the uncached input rate, cache reads keep a 90% cached-input discount, with a 30-minute minimum cache life. Availability per that page: gradual rollout over ~24 hours; Plus/Pro/Business/Enterprise get Sol in chat (Sol Pro for Pro/Enterprise); ChatGPT Work and Codex users (including Free/Go) get Sol, Terra, and Luna; API adds Programmatic Tool Calling in the Responses API and a beta multi-agent mode that runs concurrent subagents in a single request (frame at 23:12). The creator's framing of the value: near Claude-Fable-level intelligence 'for way cheaper' (01:17-01:25).

Speed: The BuseyBench #1 run (GPT-5.6 Sol Pro) cost $0.77, used 45,000 tokens, and took about a minute — vs GPT-5.5 Pro's 6 minutes and $4 for the same task (03:09-03:21). One-shot agentic builds: a Mega Bonk-style 3D shooter clone in 31 minutes from a single prompt (06:29-06:33) and a 35-minute 'impress me' website on Soul Ultra (08:13-08:15).

Benchmark numbers: Deep SWE (software engineering) 72.7%, a new leader, vs Fable's 66 and Grok 4.5's 62 (19:57-20:07). Terminal Bench: GPT-5.6 at 88 with Soul Ultra at 91.9%, vs Fable 84 and Grok 83 — best at agentic terminal use per those benchmarks (20:10-20:26). GDPval (economically valuable tasks): Sol outperforms everything except Claude Fable, which leads only narrowly (02:10-02:29). Browser/computer use: beats everything including Mythos and Fable (02:29-02:35). GPQA: ties Mythos (02:35-02:40). BuseyBench (SVG-portrait, LLM-judged): takes the top four spots — Sol Pro #1 (7.0), Terra Pro #2 (6.8), Terra #3 (6.7), Sol #4 (6.4) (02:46-03:03; leaderboard frames at 02:46-03:04).

Capabilities observed: strong one-shot game/website coding (01:33-02:02, 06:26-07:14 — better first-try Mega Bonk clone than Fable, including working mouse-look Fable needed a follow-up prompt for, 07:00-07:14); slide-deck generation that follows reference-deck templates (frames at 02:01-02:03); code review that caught security vulnerabilities (an API-key exposure) Fable missed in his Future Tools build (36:18-36:38); tasks in the new app execute in the cloud rather than locally (30:36-30:39). His subjective calibration, offered as opinion: Fable still feels smarter at architectural decisions, while 5.6 is better at 'dump everything in and walk away' completeness (35:12-35:47).

Excluded as hype/filler: 'monster leap,' 'should have been GPT-6,' 'only benchmark that really matters,' 'blowing my mind,' 'most fun in a year and a half' etc. (00:57-01:00, 02:49-02:51, 35:06-36:55) — vibes, not specs.
control.evidence_used: Transcript plus frames: OpenAI GPT-5.6 blog pages (t=01:10-02:03, t=03:24), pricing/availability page (t=23:12), BuseyBench leaderboard frames (t=02:46-03:09, t=21:06-21:36)

control.answer:
The 'Super App' is OpenAI's newly announced ChatGPT Work / unified ChatGPT app. Per the video (03:35-03:55), OpenAI is ending the separate Codex app, ChatGPT app, and Atlas browser and merging all three into a single app just called the ChatGPT app — 'essentially the super app that they've been talking about' (03:42-03:44).

What it does:
- Two modes via a dropdown: Codex mode for explicit software development (branches, repo targets, a review option and a terminal in the right sidebar, 04:50-05:32) and Work mode, a less-technical personal-assistant mode: you say what you want and it decides whether to just answer, connect to a tool/plugin, or go build something for you (04:26-04:46) — compared to Claude Co-work (04:48). Work mode's sidebar has just a browser and files (05:07-05:11).
- Built-in browser: Atlas is being wrapped up and its browser now lives inside ChatGPT (05:13-05:18).
- Model access: the 5.6 Sol, Terra, and Luna models plus 5.5, with an advanced toggle or a simple slider from 5.6 Terra Light (cheapest) to 5.6 Soul Ultra (best, uses more credits) (05:36-05:58).
- Sites: a button to prompt entire websites into existence, fully hosted on chatgpt.site — no databases, CDNs, or hosting to manage — with share/publish controls to make them public (05:58-06:12, 07:18-07:56). Demo: his one-prompt 'Soulbon' Mega Bonk clone (built in 31 minutes, 06:29-06:33) ran on localhost inside the app's browser, then was moved to Sites and published for anyone (06:35-07:56).
- Side chat: a Chat button opens a sidebar so you can ask a quick question of a chosen model without leaving the project in the main window (09:12-09:44).
- Personal-assistant/agent features in Work mode: it connects to your apps (Gmail, Google Drive, Slack, Granola, calendar, plus local projects and ChatGPT history, 09:53-10:08); in his demo it reviewed 30 days of Gmail, his calendar May 8-Aug 8, Slack DMs, 22 Granola meeting notes, 37 Drive docs, and his second brain, then produced a priority list and offered what it 'should own' for him — a daily control tower, post-meeting decision extraction and document drafting, sponsorship and editorial help (10:13-11:20). Tasks can be made recurring via a scheduled section (11:22-11:29). It also visualized his Obsidian second brain (212 saved sources, 426 wiki pages, 28 journal entries, 14 memory pages, 11:37-11:47) and extended his audience dashboard into a 'control tower' with today/AI-intel/brand-awareness tabs (12:05-12:53).
- Cloud execution: work runs in the cloud rather than locally, so tasks keep going and can be managed from your phone (30:36-30:42, 31:07-31:11).

Frames at 03:57 and 07:59 show the app UI (ChatGPT Codex window with sidebar projects, plugins, sites, scheduled sections and the impress-me website task).
control.evidence_used: Transcript 03:35-12:53 and 30:26-31:11; app UI frames at t=03:57 (ChatGPT Codex app) and t=07:59 (impress-me website task inside the app)

candidate.answer: This is a weekly AI-news roundup dominated by OpenAI launches. Main stories: (1) GPT-5.6 release - OpenAI's new flagship, described as a 'monster leap' over 5.5 [00:15, 01:00]. It ships in tiers (Soul, Terra, Luna, plus Pro/Ultra variants). Benchmarks: near Claude Fable on GDPval, best at browser/computer use, ties Mythos on GPQA [02:10-02:40]; takes the top 4 spots on Buseybench, with 5.6 Soul Pro #1 generating its entry for $0.77 / 45,000 tokens / ~1 minute vs GPT-5.5 Pro's 6 minutes and $4 [02:46-03:24]. Demo apps include a 3JS game and Clockwork Village [01:33-02:02]. Per-token pricing shown: Soul $5 in/$30 out, Terra $2.50/$15, Luna $1/$6 per million [23:12-23:20]. (2) ChatGPT Work / the new unified ChatGPT app - the 'super app' merging the Codex app, ChatGPT app, and Atlas browser into one, with Work and Codex modes [03:38-04:07], built-in browser and files [05:05-05:18], a model slider from 5.6 Terra Light to Soul Ultra [05:43-05:58], a Sites feature that builds and hosts websites (his 'Soulbon' game clone built in 31 minutes from one prompt, hosted on chatgpt.site and publishable) [05:58-07:56], an 'impress me' website test (35 min on Soul Ultra) [07:58-09:06], a side chat panel for quick questions [09:24-09:44], and a Work-mode personal-assistant demo that scanned his Gmail, calendar, Slack, Granola notes, Drive, and second brain, proposed a 'daily control tower,' visualized his Obsidian second brain (212 sources, 426 wiki pages), and built him a dashboard [09:44-12:55]. (3) Sponsor segment: HyperAgent (from the Airtable company) - build teams of AI agents, new skills marketplace, and the host's B-roll generator skill that routes prompts to VO or HyperFrames and assembles video with ffmpeg [12:55-16:14]. (4) GPT Live - new voice mode with natural two-way interruption and real-time translation (e.g., English-Hindi), demoed live [16:14-18:41]. (5) xAI Grok 4.5 - surprisingly competitive on Deep SWE and Terminal Bench, installable free CLI, $2/M input and $6/M output [18:45-23:20]. (6) Meta Muse Spark 1.1 - decent model (Terminal Bench 80, Deep SWE 53.3), $1.25/M in and $4.25/M out, $20 free credits, a big leap from Llama Scout [23:28-26:14]. (7) Meta Muse Image - controversial free image model that connects to Instagram and can generate images of anyone you @mention, with an opt-out setting not yet available to everyone [27:40-29:02]. (8) Joke plug: the new Future Tools website [29:05]. (9) Rapid-fire Anthropic news: Fable access extended from July 7 to July 12 with weekly limits reset on July 9 [29:28-30:16]; Claude Co-work coming to mobile and web with cloud execution [30:26-31:11]; a 'reflect' usage-recap feature (Spotify-wrapped-style) [31:11-31:37]; and JSpace research into the AI's 'subconscious' thought patterns [31:38-32:45]. (10) Google Photos video remix using Gemini Omni on AI Plus Pro/Ultra plans [32:45-33:01]. (11) ByteDance Seedream 5.0 Pro image model - infographics, interactive editing, Photoshop-like layers, photorealism, available in Leonardo [33:01-34:00]. He closes comparing Fable vs GPT-5.6 (Fable smarter architecturally; 5.6 better at long autonomous runs and even caught security vulnerabilities Fable missed on his Future Tools build) [35:10-36:38].
candidate.evidence_used: Full transcript plus all 16 uniform anchor frames (e.g., OpenAI benchmark tables at 02:34, ChatGPT Work UI at 05:09, Soulbon build log at 07:44, productivity audit at 10:18, control-tower dashboard at 12:53, Creator Micro 2 sponsor card at 15:28, ChatGPT voice UI at 18:02, Grok 4.5 announcement at 20:37, OpenAI GPT-5.6 pricing/rollout page at 23:12, Buseybench top-4 leaderboard at 25:46, Meta AI image of the host at 28:21, Claude Cowork blog at 30:56, Seedream layers page at 33:30).

candidate.answer: Concrete facts from the evidence, hype stripped out: (1) Availability: GPT-5.6 came out of preview on Thursday, July 9 [18:38, 30:08]. OpenAI's announcement page pitches it as 'more intelligence from every token, stronger performance per dollar, and more capability on demand' (frame at 03:25). (2) Model lineup: it ships in multiple tiers - Soul (top, with Soul Pro and Soul Ultra variants) down to Terra, the lowest-end/cheapest 5.6 model, which sits in a similar price range to Grok 4.5 and Meta's Muse Spark [03:09, 20:28, 24:35]. (3) Speed and cost datapoint: the best-performing variant, GPT-5.6 Soul Pro, generated its top benchmark entry for $0.77 using 45,000 tokens in about 1 minute, versus GPT-5.5 Pro which took 6 minutes and cost $4 for the same task - roughly 5x cheaper and 6x faster [03:09-03:24]. (4) Benchmark capability: 5.6 Soul Ultra scores 91.9% on terminal bench, making 5.6 'the best at agentic terminal use and software engineering' per those benchmarks [20:28-20:26 window]. (5) Shipped alongside it (same week, related product surface): the new ChatGPT app adds a side-chat panel so you can ask questions without leaving your project [09:26-09:44], a Work mode that acts as a personal assistant connecting to Gmail, Google Drive, Slack, Granola, etc. [09:44-09:59], and cloud execution so tasks keep running when your laptop sleeps and can be managed from your phone [30:34-30:53]. GPT Live, the new voice mode, supports natural two-way interruption and real-time spoken translation (e.g., English-Hindi) [16:14-16:47]. Caveats: the presenter's 'monster leap' framing is subjective impression, excluded here. My Q2 evidence windows did not include the exact per-million-token prices for the 5.6 tiers, so I can't state those numbers under this question's evidence rules.
candidate.evidence_used: Only the 8 q2_targeted_hype manifest windows and frames: windows at 03:09-03:40 (cost/speed vs 5.5 Pro), 09:26-09:57 (side chat, work-mode assistant), 16:14-16:47 (GPT Live), 18:21-18:52 (out of preview today), 20:28-20:59 (91.9% Soul Ultra, best agentic/SWE), 24:35-25:06 (Terra = lowest-end tier price range), 30:20-30:53 (July 9 launch date, cloud execution); frame span_03m25.jpg shows the OpenAI GPT-5.6 announcement page tagline.

candidate.answer: The 'super app' is ChatGPT Work, OpenAI's new unified ChatGPT app: instead of a separate Codex app, ChatGPT app, and Atlas browser, all three are merged into one app just called the ChatGPT app [03:38-03:55]. What it does: (1) Two modes via a dropdown - Codex mode and Work mode - though the presenter's complaint is that the UI difference between them isn't very clear [04:05-04:15]. (2) Built-in browser and files: Atlas is being wrapped up as a standalone product and its browser now lives inside ChatGPT; in Work mode the right sidebar shows Browser and Files, while Codex mode adds coding-oriented options like Review and a Terminal [05:18-05:34]. (3) Model access: inside the app you get the new 5.6 Soul, Terra, and Luna models plus 5.5, and a simpler non-advanced mode with a slider - all the way down uses 5.6 Terra Light (smallest), all the way up uses 5.6 Soul Ultra (most capable, burns more credits) [05:36-05:55]. (4) A chat button that opens a side chat panel so you can ask ChatGPT a quick question (picking your model) without leaving the project you're working on [09:14-09:26]. (5) Cloud execution: when you ask it to do stuff, the work runs in the cloud instead of locally, so it doesn't stop when your computer sleeps and you can manage it from your phone - a capability the presenter notes was 'rolled into this new chat GPT app' [31:05-31:11]. The frames show the app's UI: a 'What should we build in futuretools?' prompt screen with Codex-mode sidebar options (Review, Terminal, Browser, Files) at 03:57 and 05:34, and an 'impress me' website being built inside the app at 09:11. Note: my Q3 evidence windows did not include the segment explaining the intended purpose split between Codex mode (development) and Work mode (personal assistant), nor the Sites hosting feature, so I'm not asserting details about those here.
candidate.evidence_used: Only the 8 q3_targeted_superapp manifest windows and frames: 03:40-04:13 (merger of Codex + ChatGPT + Atlas into one app, work/codex dropdown), 05:18-05:49 (Atlas wrapped up, browser/files sidebar, review/terminal in codex mode, 5.6 Soul/Terra/Luna access, slider from Terra Light to Soul Ultra), 08:55-09:26 (chat sidebar button), 30:53-31:23 (cloud execution rolled into the new app); frames span_03m57.jpg and span_05m34.jpg show the app UI, span_09m11.jpg shows a build inside the app.
